title: Find brand and campaign assets (@explore subagent)

data.text:
I'm planning the launch + upgrade campaign for a full-service brand marketing agency whose product is the "Marketing Hub" app. I need to inventory all existing BRAND, COPY, CONTENT, and CAMPAIGN assets so we can capitalize on what already exists.

Search across /home/uzer (focus on /home/uzer/marketing-hub, /home/uzer/marketing-hub-public, /home/uzer/empire, /home/uzer/Downloads) and report:

1. BRAND IDENTITY: How is the agency/product branded? Look for: product/agency name (is it "BAZ Marketing Hub"? "BAZ"? "Midnight Terminal"? something else?), logos, color tokens, font/typography, taglines, mission/positioning statements. Read /home/uzer/empire/midnight-terminal for the design system. Find any brand guidelines docs.
2. PUBLIC-FACING PAGES: landing/marketing pages in the app (home page, pricing, features, about, contact, case studies). List routes and what they contain. Check `app/(marketing)` or similar, and `marketing-hub-public`.
3. COPY & CONTENT: find copy generators, templates, testimonials, newsletter content (`lib/marketingDiveNewsletters.ts`, `marketingDiveLibrary.ts`, `marketingDive.ts`), blog/content files, email templates, social content.
4. DESIGN PROMPTS / SKILLS: read the `docs/design-skills/prompts/**` tree (register, login pages etc.) — what UI/design assets exist?
5. EXISTING CAMPAIGN/LAUNCH DOCS: search for any markdown/docs mentioning launch, campaign, GTM, go-to-market, roadmap, marketing plan. Check /home/uzer/empire/**/AGENTS.md and any README/DEPLOY docs (there's a DEPLOY.md referenced about `roi-marketing.vercel.app` / "Pivot Studio").
6. DEPLOY/DELIVERY: the app deploys to Vercel `https://marketing-hub-roan.vercel.app`. Note any deploy config, domains, environment setup.

Report a structured markdown inventory grouped by the 6 categories above, with file paths. Highlight what is strong/ready-to-use vs what is thin/missing for a launch campaign. Do NOT edit files — research only.

The user wants a comprehensive inventory of marketing assets across several directories. Let me systematically search through the specified paths. I'll start by exploring the directory structure and then dive into specific locations.

Let me begin with broad searches across all the specified directories.

<content>
1: # Deploying Marketing Hub — Supabase + Vercel (proper setup)
2: 
3: Marketing Hub runs on **Next.js**, deployed to **Vercel**, with **Supabase** (Postgres)
4: as the production database. Locally it falls back to `better-sqlite3`; in production
5: (serverless) it **must** use Supabase — `better-sqlite3` cannot persist on Vercel.
6: 
7: A production guard in `src/lib/db-adapter.ts` throws at runtime if Vercel is detected
8: but Supabase env vars are missing, so a misconfigured deploy fails loud instead of
9: silently using a broken SQLite path.
10: 
11: ## Project facts (already set up)
12: - **Supabase project ref:** `fgjxkexgopllieggucws` → project URL `https://fgjxkexgopllieggucws.supabase.co` (already `supabase link`ed; see `supabase/.temp/project-ref`).
13: - **Vercel:** framework `nextjs`, build `next build`, install `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` (uses **pnpm**, lockfile `pnpm-lock.yaml`).
14: - **Migrations:** `supabase/migrations/0000{1..4}_*.sql` (initial schema → department upgrade → agency ops → RLS + seed).
15: - **Env files** (`.env.local`/`.env.production`/`.env.vercel`) are gitignored — safe.
16: 
17: ## 1. Get Supabase keys
18: In the Supabase dashboard → **Settings → API** for project `fgjxkexgopllieggucws`, copy:
19: - **Project URL** → `https://fgjxkexgopllieggucws.supabase.co`
20: - **anon public** key
21: - **service_role** key (secret — used by `src/lib/finance/supabase-finance.ts`)
22: 
23: ## 2. Apply migrations to Supabase (review first)
24: ```bash
25: supabase link --project-ref fgjxkexgopllieggucws   # already linked; re-run if needed
26: supabase db push                                  # applies supabase/migrations/*.sql
27: ```
28: Review the 4 migrations before pushing to a project that already has data.
29: 
30: ## 3. Set production env vars in Vercel
31: Via Vercel dashboard (Project → Settings → Environment Variables → Production) **or** CLI:
32: ```bash
33: vercel env add SUPABASE_URL production            # https://fgjxkexgopllieggucws.supabase.co
34: vercel env add SUPABASE_ANON_KEY production       # anon public key
35: vercel env add SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY production  # service_role key (SECRET)
36: vercel env add MARKETING_HUB_PUBLIC_URL production   # https://roi-marketing.vercel.app (or your domain)
37: vercel env add MARKETING_HUB_PUBLIC_HOST production  # roi.marketing (or your host)
38: # Optional: ADMIN_EMAIL / ADMIN_NAME / ADMIN_PASSWORD (admin bootstrap) — or leave blank to use /setup
39: # Optional: Stripe keys (STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, etc.) if billing is enabled — see .env.example
40: ```
41: 
42: ## 4. Deploy
43: ```bash
44: vercel deploy --prod        # or push to the linked git branch → Vercel auto-deploys
45: ```
46: 
47: ## 5. Verify
48: - Visit the production URL.
49: - Vercel logs should **not** contain the `[db-adapter] Production (Vercel) requires…` error
50:   (that error fires only if SUPABASE_URL/SUPABASE_ANON_KEY are unset → means env vars aren't set).
51: - API routes under `/api/finance/*` should read/write Supabase (not the local SQLite fallback).
52: 
53: ## Notes
54: - **pnpm** is the package manager (don't use npm/yarn — `vercel.json` installCommand is pnpm).
55: - **`next.config.js`** sets `typescript.ignoreBuildErrors` + `eslint.ignoreDuringBuilds: true` (deploy-friendly) and externalizes `better-sqlite3` (used only in local dev).
56: - The Supabase client is **server-side only** (API routes), so `SUPABASE_URL`/`SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` (not `NEXT_PUBLIC_*`) are correct. `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` is privileged — never expose it client-side.
57: - Build is heavy; run `pnpm build` locally to confirm deploy-readiness (can take a few minutes; needs ample RAM).

(End of file - total 57 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
# Deploying Marketing Hub — Supabase + Vercel (proper setup)

Marketing Hub runs on **Next.js**, deployed to **Vercel**, with **Supabase** (Postgres)
as the production database. Locally it falls back to `better-sqlite3`; in production
(serverless) it **must** use Supabase — `better-sqlite3` cannot persist on Vercel.

A production guard in `src/lib/db-adapter.ts` throws at runtime if Vercel is detected
but Supabase env vars are missing, so a misconfigured deploy fails loud instead of
silently using a broken SQLite path.

## Project facts (already set up)
- **Supabase project ref:** `fgjxkexgopllieggucws` → project URL `https://fgjxkexgopllieggucws.supabase.co` (already `supabase link`ed; see `supabase/.temp/project-ref`).
- **Vercel:** framework `nextjs`, build `next build`, install `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` (uses **pnpm**, lockfile `pnpm-lock.yaml`).
- **Migrations:** `supabase/migrations/0000{1..4}_*.sql` (initial schema → department upgrade → agency ops → RLS + seed).
- **Env files** (`.env.local`/`.env.production`/`.env.vercel`) are gitignored — safe.

## 1. Get Supabase keys
In the Supabase dashboard → **Settings → API** for project `fgjxkexgopllieggucws`, copy:
- **Project URL** → `https://fgjxkexgopllieggucws.supabase.co`
- **anon public** key

<path>/home/uzer/marketing-hub/README.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Marketing Hub — All-in-One Marketing Command Center
2: 
3: > ⚡ **Core Doctrine: Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time.**
4: > One channel mastered beats five channels touched. One offer sharpened beats ten offers scattered. One persona deeply understood beats five personas guessed. Read the full principle in [`docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md`](docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md).
5: 
6: A complete marketing platform built with **Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind, and SQLite**. **30 interconnected sections** covering the entire marketing workflow — all running locally, no external API keys required.
7: 
8: ## Quick start
9: 
10: ```bash
11: cd ~/marketing-hub
12: npm install
13: npm run seed               # populate with 200+ sample records
14: npm run dev                # http://localhost:3000
15: ```
16: 
17: For production:
18: ```bash
19: npm run build
20: npm start
21: ```
22: 
23: ## What's inside (30 sections)
24: 
25: ### 🧭 Plan
26: | Section | What it does |
27: |---|---|
28: | **Dashboard** | Unified metrics, module grid, traffic/conversion charts, active campaigns, upcoming content |
29: | **Brand kit** | Logos (primary/dark/icon), color palette, typography, voice & tone, usage guidelines |
30: | **Personas** | Audience profiles with goals, pain points, channels, messaging. Primary / secondary / anti-persona tiers |
31: | **Segments** | Rule-based audience segments (e.g. `score > 70 AND tag = newsletter`) |
32: | **Competitors** | Track competitors: positioning, pricing, SWOT analysis, strengths & weaknesses |
33: | **Campaigns** | Kanban (draft → scheduled → live → paused → completed), full CRUD, detail page, budget tracking |
34: | **Funnels** | Visual customer-journey builder — map awareness → landing → form → email → sale. Tracks conversion per step |
35: | **Budget planner** | Monthly budget by category, planned vs actual, variance tracking, category breakdown chart |
36: | **Playbooks** | Repeatable SOPs with steps, owners, durations, KPIs. Acquisition / activation / retention / revenue / branding |
37: 
38: ### ✏️ Create
39: | Section | What it does |
40: |---|---|
41: | **Content calendar** | Monthly grid view with per-channel color coding, click-to-create |
42: | **Social studio** | Composer with per-channel character limits (Twitter 280, LinkedIn 3000, etc.), preview, scheduling |
43: | **Email builder** | Block-based editor (hero, features, CTA, testimonials, pricing, FAQ), live preview, HTML/text export |
44: | **Landing pages** | Same block editor for marketing pages, live preview, SEO meta, static HTML export |
45: | **Forms** | Drag-style form builder — text/email/phone/textarea/select/checkbox/number/URL fields, submit tracking |
46: | **Lead magnets** | Library of eBooks, templates, checklists, webinars, coupons, videos — track downloads |
47: | **Copy generator** | Offline template-based: headlines, hooks, CTAs, ad copy, social posts, email sequences, blog outlines |
48: | **Asset library** | File upload, image/video/document previews, search, tags |
49: 
50: ### 📈 Grow
51: | Section | What it does |
52: |---|---|
53: | **SEO toolkit** | Keyword tracker (volume/difficulty/opportunity), HTML auditor, sitemap.xml, robots.txt |
54: | **Ads manager** | Multi-channel (Meta/Google/TikTok/LinkedIn), spend/impressions/clicks/CTR/CPA, pause/resume |
55: | **Events & webinars** | Webinars, conferences, meetups, workshops, launches — registration tracking, capacity fill rate |
56: | **CRM & deals** | Contacts with scoring, companies, deals kanban (drag between stages), activity tracking |
57: | **Automations** | Trigger→action rule builder (8 triggers × 8 actions), run tracking, pause/resume |
58: | **A/B testing** | Experiments with two variants, traffic & conversions, auto-computed lift & confidence, winner flag |
59: | **Testimonials** | Social proof library with ratings, sources (G2/Trustpilot/Twitter/email/manual), featured toggle, avatars |
60: | **Conversations inbox** | Unified inbox — DMs, comments, replies, emails. Channel filters, sentiment badges, star, search, reply |
61: | **Surveys (NPS, CSAT)** | NPS (0-10), CSAT (1-5), CES (1-7), custom — track responses & aggregate score |
62: | **Analytics** | Multi-range charts (traffic/conversions/revenue), funnel viz, channel mix, per-channel performance |
63: 
64: ### ⚙️ Manage
65: | Section | What it does |
66: |---|---|
67: | **Tasks** | To-do tracker with priority, due date, project tagging |
68: | **Integrations** | 24 service connectors (Mailchimp, SendGrid, HubSpot, Meta, Google, Slack, etc.) with connect/disconnect flow |
69: | **Reports** | Auto-generated performance summaries, CSV export, jsPDF PDF export |
70: | **Settings** | Workspace, brand color, email defaults, API key vault (encrypted at rest) |
71: 
72: ## Architecture
73: 
74: - **Next.js 14 App Router** with server components + client islands
75: - **SQLite** (`better-sqlite3`) for everything — single-file database at `data/hub.sqlite`
76: - **Tailwind CSS** + custom design system (no UI library), dynamic color safelist
77: - **Recharts** for all charts
78: - **lucide-react** for icons
79: - **framer-motion** for premium animations
80: - **jsPDF** for PDF reports
81: - **TypeScript** strict mode throughout
82: - RESTful API routes under `/api/*`
83: - Full CRUD on every resource
84: - Auto-migrations on first run (creates 35 tables)
85: 
86: ## Database schema (35 tables)
87: 
88: `users, workspaces, settings, contacts, companies, deals, activities, campaigns, content, emails, landing_pages, seo_keywords, seo_audits, ads, assets, automations, integrations, api_keys, copy_templates, notes, tasks, brand_assets, personas, segments, forms, form_submissions, lead_magnets, funnels, experiments, testimonials, events, event_registrations, competitors, conversations, budget_items, playbooks, surveys, survey_responses`
89: 
90: ## File map
91: 
92: ```
93: src/
94: ├── app/                              # 35 page routes
95: │   ├── page.tsx                      # Dashboard with module grid
96: │   ├── brand/                        # Brand kit (logos, colors, type, voice)
97: │   ├── personas/                     # Audience personas
98: │   ├── segments/                     # Rule-based segments
99: │   ├── forms/                        # Form builder
100: │   ├── lead-magnets/                 # eBooks, templates, etc.
101: │   ├── funnels/                      # Visual customer journeys
102: │   ├── experiments/                  # A/B testing
103: │   ├── testimonials/                 # Social proof
104: │   ├── events/                       # Webinars & conferences
105: │   ├── competitors/                  # Competitive intelligence + SWOT
106: │   ├── inbox/                        # Unified conversations
107: │   ├── budget/                       # Spend planner
108: │   ├── playbooks/                    # SOPs
109: │   ├── surveys/                      # NPS, CSAT, CES
110: │   ├── campaigns/[id]/page.tsx       # Campaign detail
111: │   ├── emails/[id]/page.tsx          # Email builder
112: │   ├── landing-pages/[id]/page.tsx   # Landing page builder
113: │   ├── preview/[id]/page.tsx         # Iframe preview
114: │   └── api/                          # 71+ API routes (full CRUD on every resource)
115: ├── components/
116: │   ├── Sidebar.tsx                   # 30 modules in 4 groups
117: │   ├── Topbar.tsx, Modal.tsx, Stat.tsx, Badge.tsx
118: │   ├── BlockEditor.tsx               # Block-based content editor
119: │   ├── CommandPalette.tsx            # ⌘K with all 30 sections
120: │   ├── useFetch.ts, Anim.tsx, Skeleton.tsx, Toast.tsx, ThemeProvider.tsx
121: └── lib/
122:     ├── db/index.ts                   # SQLite + 35-table schema + auto-migrations
123:     ├── api-helpers.ts                # listRows, insertRow, updateRow, deleteRow
124:     ├── constants.ts                  # Channels, deal stages, integrations, formatters
125:     ├── blocks.ts                     # Block model + HTML renderers
126:     ├── copy.ts                       # Copy generation (headlines, hooks, CTAs, etc.)
127:     ├── seo.ts                        # SEO audit, sitemap, robots
128:     └── analytics.ts                  # Deterministic mock data for charts
129: 
130: scripts/seed.ts                       # Seeds 250+ records across all 30 sections
131: data/hub.sqlite                       # SQLite database (auto-created)
132: ```
133: 
134: ## Verified working
135: 
136: All 35 pages and 71+ APIs return 200. Tested:
137: - Dashboard, brand kit, personas, segments, forms, lead magnets, funnels, experiments, testimonials, events, competitors, inbox, budget, playbooks, surveys
138: - All existing: campaigns, calendar, studio, emails, landing pages, copy, SEO, ads, CRM, analytics, automations, assets, integrations, reports, settings, tasks
139: - Detail pages (campaigns/[id], emails/[id], landing-pages/[id], preview/[id])
140: - Create / Read / Update / Delete on every new resource
141: - Form submissions increment leads_count
142: - Survey responses auto-recompute aggregate score
143: - A/B test lift + confidence auto-computed
144: - ⌘K palette shows all 30 sections + searches across all records
145: - Module grid on dashboard with live counts from each section
146: 
147: ## Coverage matrix
148: 
149: | Marketing area | Hub coverage |
150: |---|---|
151: | Strategy & planning | Brand kit, Personas, Competitors, Playbooks, Funnels |
152: | Audience & segmentation | Personas, Segments, CRM |
153: | Content creation | Calendar, Studio, Email builder, Landing pages, Forms, Copy generator |
154: | Distribution & channels | Social, Email, Landing pages, Forms, Events, Inbox |
155: | Lead capture | Forms, Lead magnets, Landing pages |
156: | Conversion & sales | Funnels, Experiments, A/B tests |
157: | Paid acquisition | Ads manager, Budget |
158: | Earned & owned | SEO, Content, Email, Events |
159: | Customer feedback | Surveys, Testimonials, Inbox |
160: | Operations & measurement | Analytics, Reports, Budget, Tasks, Integrations |
161: 
162: → **30 sections, 35 DB tables, 71 API routes, 250+ seeded records.**

(End of file - total 162 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
# Marketing Hub — All-in-One Marketing Command Center

> ⚡ **Core Doctrine: Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time.**
> One channel mastered beats five channels touched. One offer sharpened beats ten offers scattered. One persona deeply understood beats five personas guessed. Read the full principle in [`docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md`](docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md).

A complete marketing platform built with **Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind, and SQLite**. **30 interconnected sections** covering the entire marketing workflow — all running locally, no external API keys required.

## Quick start

```bash
cd ~/marketing-hub
npm install
npm run seed               # populate with 200+ sample records
npm run dev                # http://localhost:3000
```

For production:
```bash
npm run build
npm start

56:   return db.prepare(`SELECT * FROM newsletter_subs WHERE user_id = ?`).all(userId) as Record<string, unknown>[];
57: }
58: 
59: export function setSub(userId: string, newsletterId: string, email: string, active: boolean) {
60:   db.exec(`CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS newsletter_subs (
61:     id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
62:     user_id TEXT NOT NULL,
63:     newsletter_id TEXT NOT NULL,
64:     email TEXT NOT NULL,
65:     active INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
66:     created_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
67:     UNIQUE(user_id, newsletter_id)
68:   );`);
69:   const id = `sub_${userId}_${newsletterId}`;
70:   db.prepare(
71:     `
72:     INSERT INTO newsletter_subs (id, user_id, newsletter_id, email, active, created_at)
73:     VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
74:     ON CONFLICT(user_id, newsletter_id) DO UPDATE SET active = excluded.active, email = excluded.email
75:   `,
76:   ).run(id, userId, newsletterId, email, active ? 1 : 0, now());
77:   return { id, active };
78: }

1: /**
2:  * Marketing Dive-style Library: gated resources — playbooks, webinars,
3:  * reports, infographics, surveys. Stored locally; the original library is
4:  * sponsored content, so we mirror that model: every resource has a sponsor
5:  * and a format.
6:  */
7: import { db, now } from "@/lib/db";
8: 
9: export const LIBRARY_FORMATS = [
10:   { id: "playbook", label: "Playbook" },
11:   { id: "webinar", label: "Webinar (on demand)" },
12:   { id: "report", label: "Industry Report" },
13:   { id: "infographic", label: "Infographic" },
14:   { id: "survey", label: "Survey Report" },
15:   { id: "trendline", label: "Trendline" },
16:   { id: "virtual-event", label: "Virtual Event Playback" },
17: ];
18: 
19: export interface LibraryItem {
20:   id: string;
21:   title: string;
22:   url: string;
23:   format: string;
24:   sponsor: string;
25:   topic: string;
26:   description: string | null;
27:   published_at: number;
28: }
29: 
30: export function listLibrary(opts: { format?: string; topic?: string; q?: string } = {}) {
31:   db.exec(`
32:     CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS dive_library (
33:       id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
34:       title TEXT NOT NULL,
35:       url TEXT NOT NULL,
36:       format TEXT NOT NULL,
37:       sponsor TEXT NOT NULL,
38:       topic TEXT NOT NULL,
39:       description TEXT,
40:       published_at INTEGER NOT NULL
41:     );
42:     CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_lib_format ON dive_library(format);
43:     CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_lib_topic ON dive_library(topic);
44:   `);
45:   const where: string[] = [];
46:   const params: Record<string, unknown>[] = [];
47:   if (opts.format && opts.format !== "all") {
48:     where.push("format = ?");
49:     params.push(opts.format);
50:   }
51:   if (opts.topic && opts.topic !== "all") {
52:     where.push("topic = ?");
53:     params.push(opts.topic);
54:   }
55:   if (opts.q) {
56:     where.push("title LIKE ?");
57:     params.push(`%${opts.q}%`);
58:   }
59:   const w = where.length ? `WHERE ${where.join(" AND ")}` : "";
60:   const rows = db
61:     .prepare(`SELECT * FROM dive_library ${w} ORDER BY published_at DESC LIMIT 100`)
62:     .all(...params) as Record<string, unknown>[];
63:   return rows;
64: }
65: 
66: export function seedLibrary(rows: Array<Omit<LibraryItem, "id" | "published_at">>) {
67:   db.exec(`
68:     CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS dive_library (
69:       id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
70:       title TEXT NOT NULL,
71:       url TEXT NOT NULL,
72:       format TEXT NOT NULL,
73:       sponsor TEXT NOT NULL,
74:       topic TEXT NOT NULL,
75:       description TEXT,
76:       published_at INTEGER NOT NULL
77:     );
78:   `);
79:   const insert = db.prepare(`
80:     INSERT OR IGNORE INTO dive_library (id, title, url, format, sponsor, topic, description, published_at)
81:     VALUES (@id, @title, @url, @format, @sponsor, @topic, @description, @published_at)
82:   `);
83:   let n = 0;
84:   const tx = db.transaction(() => {
85:     for (const r of rows) {
86:       const id = `lib_${r.title
87:         .toLowerCase()
88:         .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "_")
89:         .slice(0, 40)}_${r.sponsor.toLowerCase().replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "")}`;
90:       const ok = insert.run({ id, ...r, published_at: now() });
91:       if (ok.changes) n++;
92:     }
93:   });
94:   tx();
95:   return n;
96: }

(End of file - total 96 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
/**
 * Marketing Dive ingest + read API.
 *
 * Scrapes marketingdive.com once, caches articles + topic pages in SQLite,
 * exposes them through the Wire so the Triangle loop can use real industry
 * intelligence when scoring/positioning.
 *
 * Topics we follow (the actual vertical taxonomy from marketingdive.com):
 *   brand-strategy · creative · mobile-marketing · social-media-marketing
 *   video-marketing · agencies · cmo-corner · analytics · marketing-tech
 *   influencer-marketing
 *
 * Formats (from real article HTML):
 *   news · opinion · column · qa · sociable · campaign-trail · deep-dive · trendline
 */
import { db, now, uid } from "@/lib/db";

export const DIVE_TOPICS = [
  { id: "brand-strategy", label: "Brand Strategy" },
  { id: "creative", label: "Creative" },

<content>
1: // Template-based copy generator. No external AI; works fully offline.
2: // Produces varied output by combining templates with random picks.
3: 
4: function pick<T>(arr: T[]): T {
5:   return arr[Math.floor(Math.random() * arr.length)];
6: }
7: function pickN<T>(arr: T[], n: number): T[] {
8:   const a = [...arr];
9:   const out: T[] = [];
10:   while (out.length < n && a.length) {
11:     out.push(a.splice(Math.floor(Math.random() * a.length), 1)[0]);
12:   }
13:   return out;
14: }
15: 
16: export interface CopyInput {
17:   topic: string;
18:   audience?: string;
19:   brand?: string;
20:   tone?: "friendly" | "professional" | "bold" | "playful" | "urgent";
21:   benefits?: string[];
22:   features?: string[];
23: }
24: 
25: const TONES = {
26:   friendly: { you: "you", pronoun: "we'd love to", cta: "Give it a try" },
27:   professional: { you: "you", pronoun: "we recommend", cta: "Learn more" },
28:   bold: { you: "you", pronoun: "you deserve", cta: "Claim yours now" },
29:   playful: { you: "you", pronoun: "let's get", cta: "Try it on" },
30:   urgent: { you: "you", pronoun: "now's the time", cta: "Don't wait" },
31: };
32: 
33: export function generateHeadlines(input: CopyInput, count = 8): string[] {
34:   const t = (input.topic || "your goals").trim();
35:   const aud = (input.audience || "teams like yours").trim();
36:   const b = input.brand?.trim();
37:   const bn = input.benefits?.[0];
38:   const tone = TONES[input.tone || "friendly"];
39:   const templates = [
40:     `${cap(t)} — without the headache`,
41:     `How ${aud} ${tone.pronoun} ${t.toLowerCase()}`,
42:     `${cap(t)}, simplified`,
43:     `The smarter way to ${t.toLowerCase()}`,
44:     `Built for ${aud}`,
45:     `Stop guessing. Start ${t.toLowerCase()}.`,
46:     bn ? `${cap(bn)} — ${t.toLowerCase()} that just works` : `${cap(t)} that actually works`,
47:     b ? `${b}: ${cap(t)} for the rest of us` : `${cap(t)}, made simple`,
48:     `What if ${t.toLowerCase()} was easy?`,
49:     `${cap(t)} for ${aud}`,
50:     `Finally — ${t.toLowerCase()} that scales`,
51:     `Less busywork. More ${t.toLowerCase()}.`,
52:     `Your shortcut to ${t.toLowerCase()}`,
53:     `Three reasons ${aud} love ${t.toLowerCase()}`,
54:   ];
55:   return pickN(templates, Math.min(count, templates.length));
56: }
57: 
58: export function generateHooks(input: CopyInput, count = 8): string[] {
59:   const t = input.topic || "this";
60:   const aud = input.audience || "most people";
61:   const bn = input.benefits?.[0];
62:   const templates = [
63:     `Here's the thing about ${t.toLowerCase()} —`,
64:     `If you're a ${aud}, read this.`,
65:     `Most people get ${t.toLowerCase()} wrong. Here's why.`,
66:     `I spent weeks figuring out ${t.toLowerCase()}. Here's what I learned:`,
67:     `A quick story about ${t.toLowerCase()}:`,
68:     `Three words changed how I think about ${t.toLowerCase()}:`,
69:     `Plot twist: ${t} doesn't have to be hard.`,
70:     bn ? `The real reason ${bn.toLowerCase()} matters:` : `The truth about ${t.toLowerCase()}:`,
71:     `If ${t.toLowerCase()} feels overwhelming, start here:`,
72:     `Here's a 60-second lesson in ${t.toLowerCase()}:`,
73:     `Unpopular opinion: most ${t.toLowerCase()} advice is wrong.`,
74:     `Steal this ${t.toLowerCase()} framework:`,
75:   ];
76:   return pickN(templates, Math.min(count, templates.length));
77: }
78: 
79: export function generateCTAs(input: CopyInput, count = 10): string[] {
80:   const t = (input.topic || "this").toLowerCase();
81:   const bn = input.benefits?.[0];
82:   const templates = [
83:     `Get started free`,
84:     `Try it now`,
85:     `See it in action`,
86:     `Start in 60 seconds`,
87:     `Claim your spot`,
88:     `Show me how`,
89:     `Book a demo`,
90:     `Send me the guide`,
91:     `Unlock ${bn || t}`,
92:     `Yes, I want this`,
93:     `Take the next step`,
94:     `Get the playbook`,
95:     `Start ${t} today`,
96:   ];
97:   return pickN(templates, Math.min(count, templates.length));
98: }
99: 
100: export function generateAdCopy(input: CopyInput): {
101:   primary: string;
102:   headline: string;
103:   description: string;
104:   cta: string;
105: } {
106:   const headlines = generateHeadlines(input, 1);
107:   const ctas = generateCTAs(input, 1);
108:   const t = input.topic || "this";
109:   const bn = input.benefits?.[0] || "real results";
110:   return {
111:     primary: `${headlines[0]}. ${bn}.`,
112:     headline: headlines[0],
113:     description: `${bn}, made simple for ${input.audience || "busy teams"}.`,
114:     cta: ctas[0],
115:   };
116: }
117: 
118: export function generateEmailSequence(
119:   input: CopyInput,
120: ): Array<{ day: number; subject: string; preheader: string; body: string }> {
121:   const t = input.topic || "this";
122:   const aud = input.audience || "you";
123:   const bn = input.benefits?.[0] || "real value";
124:   const brand = input.brand || "We";
125:   return [
126:     {
127:       day: 0,
128:       subject: `Welcome — let's get you started with ${t}`,
129:       preheader: `Quick wins in your first 5 minutes`,
130:       body: `Hi {{first_name}},\n\nWelcome aboard. Here's the fastest path to ${bn.toLowerCase()} with ${t}.\n\n1. Do this one thing first\n2. Then this\n3. Watch the magic\n\nReply if you get stuck — we read every message.\n\n— ${brand}`,
131:     },
132:     {
133:       day: 2,
134:       subject: `The one mistake ${aud} make with ${t}`,
135:       preheader: `And how to avoid it`,
136:       body: `Hi {{first_name}},\n\nQuick one — most ${aud} jump into ${t} without this step, and it costs them hours.\n\nThe fix is simple: [describe].\n\nTry it today and let me know how it goes.\n\n— ${brand}`,
137:     },
138:     {
139:       day: 5,
140:       subject: `A shortcut for ${t}`,
141:       preheader: `Save this for later`,
142:       body: `Hi {{first_name}},\n\nSaw you checking things out — here's a shortcut we use internally:\n\n[TIP]. It cuts the time for ${t} in half.\n\n— ${brand}`,
143:     },
144:     {
145:       day: 7,
146:       subject: `Still thinking it over?`,
147:       preheader: `Last note from me`,
148:       body: `Hi {{first_name}},\n\nNo pressure — just wanted to ask: is there anything blocking you from getting started with ${t}?\n\nIf I can help, hit reply.\n\n— ${brand}`,
149:     },
150:   ];

<path>/home/uzer/marketing-hub/docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time
2: 
3: > **The core marketing doctrine for all projects.**
4: 
5: ---
6: 
7: ## The Principle
8: 
9: **One channel, mastered deeply, will outperform five channels covered shallowly. Every. Single. Time.**
10: 
11: This isn't a preference. It's a law of focus. Marketing success comes from:
12: 
13: 1. **Depth of message** — One offer, refined to razor sharpness, hitting the exact nerve of the exact persona
14: 2. **Depth of channel** — One platform understood at the algorithm level, not the surface level
15: 3. **Depth of frequency** — Showing up consistently in one place until you're unavoidable, not flickering across many
16: 4. **Depth of creative** — One piece of content so good it travels on its own, not ten pieces that need pushing
17: 
18: ## What This Means in Practice
19: 
20: ### ❌ Extensity (what we don't do)
21: - Launching on 7 social platforms simultaneously
22: - Running Google Ads + Meta Ads + TikTok + LinkedIn Ads all at once with thin budgets
23: - Writing blog posts for SEO, social posts for engagement, email sequences for nurture, and a podcast for authority — all half-baked
24: - Spreading $5K/month across 10 channels ($500 each, none getting traction)
25: - Copy-pasting the same content across 6 platforms with minor tweaks
26: 
27: ### ✅ Intensity (what we do)
28: - **Pick ONE channel** that matches where the persona actually lives
29: - **Pour the full budget** into that one channel until it compounds
30: - **Create ONE hero piece** of content per week so good it would be embarrassing to competitor
31: - **Run ONE ad campaign** with enough daily spend to get statistical significance in days, not months
32: - **Build ONE funnel** and optimize every step until conversion is best-in-class
33: - **Dominate one keyword cluster** before touching a second
34: 
35: ## The Intensity Checklist
36: 
37: Before approving any marketing project, ask:
38: 
39: - [ ] **Is this focused on ONE primary channel?** If more than two, cut.
40: - [ ] **Is the budget concentrated enough to generate real signal?** Thin spreads produce noise, not data.
41: - [ ] **Is there ONE hero offer being pushed?** Multiple offers dilute attention and confuse tracking.
42: - [ ] **Is the creative quality high enough to win on its own merit?** If it needs paid distribution to be seen, it's not intense enough.
43: - [ ] **Are we going deep on one persona before expanding?** Message-market fit with one audience before adding a second.
44: - [ ] **Is there a single primary KPI we're optimizing for?** Not 12 vanity metrics. One number that matters.
45: 
46: ## How This Applies to the Marketing Hub
47: 
48: The Marketing Hub has 30 sections. That's the toolkit. But **no single campaign should activate all 30 simultaneously.**
49: 
50: | Phase | Intensity Focus | What We Use |
51: |---|---|---|
52: | **Phase 1: Dominate** | Pick the highest-ROI channel + persona combo | Personas, Campaigns, Copy Generator, one channel in Social Studio |
53: | **Phase 2: Convert** | Build the world's best funnel for that channel | Landing Pages, Forms, A/B Testing, CRM, Automations |
54: | **Phase 3: Measure** | Know that one channel better than anyone | Analytics, Attribution, SEO Toolkit (for organic) or Ads Manager (for paid) |
55: | **Phase 4: Expand** | ONLY after channel #1 is compounding, add channel #2 | Repeat Phase 1-3 for new channel |
56: 
57: ## The Anti-Pattern We Reject
58: 
59: > "We need to be everywhere our customers might be."
60: 
61: No. You need to be **unmissable** in the one place they definitely are. Being everywhere means being nowhere. Being nowhere means being forgotten. Being forgotten means being irrelevant.
62: 
63: ## The Rule of One
64: 
65: - **One** persona to start
66: - **One** channel to dominate
67: - **One** offer to push
68: - **One** hero creative per cycle
69: - **One** KPI to optimize
70: - **One** funnel to perfect
71: 
72: Master the one. Then — and only then — add the second.
73: 
74: ---
75: 
76: *This principle governs all marketing projects, campaigns, and strategies. When in doubt, go deeper, not wider.*

(End of file - total 76 lines)
</content>

---

## The Principle

**One channel, mastered deeply, will outperform five channels covered shallowly. Every. Single. Time.**

This isn't a preference. It's a law of focus. Marketing success comes from:

1. **Depth of message** — One offer, refined to razor sharpness, hitting the exact nerve of the exact persona
2. **Depth of channel** — One platform understood at the algorithm level, not the surface level
3. **Depth of frequency** — Showing up consistently in one place until you're unavoidable, not flickering across many
4. **Depth of creative** — One piece of content so good it travels on its own, not ten pieces that need pushing

## What This Means in Practice

### ❌ Extensity (what we don't do)

1: # BIG TECH AUDIT — Critiques & Solutions
2: 
3: ## How a panel of senior designers from Google, Apple, Meta, Stripe, and Linear would review this product, and how we addressed every concern.
4: 
5: ---
6: 
7: ## CRITIQUE 1: "No unified design system" — Google Material Design team
8: 
9: **The critique:** "Your components are styled ad-hoc across 153 files. There's no single source of truth for design tokens, component patterns, or animation language. A new designer would need weeks to understand your system."
10: 
11: **Our solution:**
12: - 21 CSS variables defined per theme via `buildVars()` helper — every token the system uses
13: - 5 complete themes (Hub, Noira Dark, Noira Light, Noira AMOLED, Agency Light)
14: - Living design system page at `/design-system` showing all tokens, components, animations, and principles
15: - Theme-aware brand color system — `from-violet-500`, `bg-brand-50`, `text-brand-600` all map to the active theme's brand color
16: - CSS animation primitives: `fadeInUp`, `scaleIn`, `slideInRight`, `float`, `glowPulse`, `gradientShift`
17: - Shadow system: 4 elevation levels (sm, md, lg, xl) with theme-aware opacity
18: 
19: ## CRITIQUE 2: "Dark mode is broken" — Apple HIG team
20: 
21: **The critique:** "Half your components use hardcoded `bg-white` and `text-slate-900`. When I switch to dark mode, cards stay white, text stays dark, gradients are purple instead of brand-colored. This wouldn't pass our design review."
22: 
23: **Our solution:**
24: - 284 `dark:` variants across 91 files
25: - 0 remaining solid `bg-white` without dark mode support
26: - Complete legacy color fallback system in `globals.css` — `bg-white`, `text-slate-*`, `border-slate-*`, `hover:bg-slate-*` all map to CSS variables
27: - Dark mode-specific overrides for: badges (brighter colors), borders (higher opacity), backgrounds (tinted), text (brighter), shadows (darker), glass effect (darker)
28: - `color-scheme: dark` set on `.dark` class for native control rendering
29: - Theme-aware gradient mesh using brand colors instead of hardcoded purple/pink
30: 
31: ## CRITIQUE 3: "No API documentation" — Stripe Developer Experience team
32: 
33: **The critique:** "You have 244 API endpoints but zero documentation. No developer portal. No code examples. No webhook event catalog. No API key management UI. This is unusable for integration teams."
34: 
35: **Our solution:**
36: - Developer portal at `/developers` with:
37:   - 6 API category sections (Core, Content, Analytics, Automation, Enterprise, Public)
38:   - 50+ documented endpoints with method, path, description, and auth requirement
39:   - Code examples in cURL, JavaScript, and Python
40:   - 16+ webhook event types catalog
41:   - Authentication guide with API key scoping
42:   - Rate limiting documentation
43: - API key system with SHA-256 hashing, scoped permissions, and last-used tracking
44: - `X-Workspace-Id` header for multi-tenant targeting
45: - Public API endpoints (no auth) for health, status, case studies, lead capture
46: 
47: ## CRITIQUE 4: "No enterprise readiness" — Meta Enterprise team
48: 
49: **The critique:** "No SSO. No audit trail. No role-based access control beyond admin. No data export. No compliance documentation. No comparison to incumbents. Enterprise procurement would reject this."
50: 
51: **Our solution:**
52: - Enterprise page at `/enterprise` addressing every concern:
53:   - Security: HttpOnly cookies, bcrypt, rate limiting, SQL injection prevention, PII hashing, GDPR consent
54:   - Enterprise: multi-workspace tenancy, RBAC (Owner/Admin/Editor/Viewer), audit logs, API keys, custom domains, data export
55:   - DX: 244 REST endpoints, webhooks, self-hosted, no vendor lock-in
56:   - Transparency: no hidden tracking, open architecture, full audit trail
57: - Comparison table vs HubSpot, Marketo, Salesforce
58: - Workspace audit log table tracking every action with user, entity, IP, timestamp
59: - Workspace invites with expiring tokens
60: - Full backup export to tar.gz
61: 
62: ## CRITIQUE 5: "No accessibility" — Microsoft Inclusive Design team
63: 
64: **The critique:** "You have 47 ARIA attributes across 153 files — that's 0.3 per file. No focus traps on modals. No keyboard navigation on custom widgets. No screen reader announcements. This fails WCAG 2.1 AA."
65: 
66: **Our solution:**
67: - Skip-to-content link in root layout
68: - `aria-current="page"` on all active sidebar links
69: - `aria-label` on all icon-only buttons (menu toggle, notifications, keyboard shortcuts)
70: - `aria-modal="true"` + `role="dialog"` on mobile sidebar drawer
71: - `aria-busy` on loading submit buttons
72: - `aria-expanded` on search combobox
73: - `role="alert"` on error messages
74: - `sr-only` class in all CSS files
75: - `color-scheme` property for native control rendering
76: - `prefers-reduced-motion` media query — all animations disabled
77: - Focus-visible ring on all interactive elements
78: - Keyboard navigation: Escape closes modals/dropdowns, ArrowUp/Down navigates search results, Enter selects
79: 
80: ## CRITIQUE 6: "No error handling" — Google SRE team
81: 
82: **The critique:** "If a page crashes, the user sees a raw stack trace. If an API fails, there's no retry logic. If the database is unreachable, the app hangs. This is not production-grade."
83: 
84: **Our solution:**
85: - `error.tsx` — global error boundary with recovery UI, dev-only stack traces, "Try again" + "Go home" buttons
86: - `loading.tsx` — skeleton loading state for all pages during navigation
87: - `not-found.tsx` — branded 404 page (SSR-safe, no framer-motion)
88: - ErrorBoundary component for Vite apps (nova-with-bank, nova-cockpit)
89: - Try/catch on critical API routes (deals, leads, auth)
90: - Rate limiting on auth endpoints with DB-logged attempts
91: - Process cleanup — SQLite WAL checkpoint + db.close() on SIGTERM/SIGINT
92: - PageTransition rewritten to CSS animations (framer-motion crashed the 404 page)
93: 
94: ## CRITIQUE 7: "No mobile experience" — Apple Mobile team
95: 
96: **The critique:** "Your sidebar is 264px fixed width on all screens. On a 375px iPhone, that's 70% of the viewport. There's no hamburger menu, no drawer, no responsive grids. This is desktop-only."
97: 
98: **Our solution:**
99: - Mobile sidebar drawer with:
100:   - `hidden lg:flex` on desktop sidebar (hidden below 1024px)

(Showing lines 1-100 of 175. Use offset=101 to continue.)
</content>

1: # Marketing Hub — Savage Mode Proposal
2: 
3: > ⚡ **Governing Principle: Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time.** Every phase, every feature, every campaign must concentrate force rather than spread it. See [`docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md`](docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md).
4: 
5: **Author:** MiniMax-M3 (via pi / Nova shell)
6: **Date:** 2026-06-24
7: **Status:** Awaiting Board approval
8: **Target tree:** `~/empire/prototype/marketing-hub/` (per Empire Rules v2)
9: 
10: ---
11: 
12: ## TL;DR
13: 
14: Turn Marketing Hub from a solid v2.0 (30 sections, 35 pages, 71 APIs, 38 tables, single-user SQLite) into **the best marketing hub ever built** — a multi-tenant, AI-native, real-time, embeddable, automated platform that can replace HubSpot + Mailchimp + Buffer + Hootsuite + ConvertKit + Intercom + Zapier for a single operator running an empire.
15: 
16: I'm proposing **6 phases over 6 weeks**, each shippable independently. Every phase ends in something you can demo to a client.
17: 
18: ---
19: 
20: ## Honest assessment of v2.0
21: 
22: **What's great**
23: - 30 sections covering the entire marketing funnel — nothing else does this as one app
24: - Beautiful, opinionated design system (no UI library dependency, fast)
25: - All local, no external API keys required to start
26: - SQLite = instant, single-file, deterministic
27: - Recharts + framer-motion = premium feel for zero infra
28: 
29: **What's holding it back from "best ever"**
30: 
31: | # | Gap | Impact |
32: |---|---|---|
33: | 1 | **Single-user, single-tenant** | Can't onboard clients, no isolation |
34: | 2 | **No auth** | Can't even protect the app from a public URL |
35: | 3 | **No real-time** | Inbox, automations, social mentions — all feel dead |
36: | 4 | **No AI** | Copy generator is template-based, content composer doesn't draft |
37: | 5 | **No actual publishing** | "Schedule" posts are aspirational DB rows |
38: | 6 | **No embeddable forms** | Forms are stuck inside the app, no way to capture leads on your real site |
39: | 7 | **No email send** | Email builder produces HTML, but never sends |
40: | 8 | **No event stream** | Can't do "user did X, then Y" automations |
41: | 9 | **No billing** | Can't charge per workspace |
42: | 10 | **No public API** | Can't integrate with anything outside |
43: | 11 | **Deterministic mock data** | Charts lie; you can't trust the analytics view |
44: | 12 | **No collaboration** | Comments, mentions, multi-editor — none of it |
45: 
46: ---
47: 
48: ## The North Star
49: 
50: > **"A sovereign operator opens one app, plans a quarter, creates a brand, drafts a campaign with AI, builds a landing page, designs an A/B test, sends emails to a real list, schedules real social posts, captures leads from an embedded form on a real site, watches the funnel populate in real time, and exports a board-ready report — all without leaving the app, all on a single machine, all theirs."**
51: 
52: If we're going to make this **the best marketing hub ever**, it has to do **all** of that, and do it better than the SaaS alternatives.
53: 
54: ---
55: 
56: ## Architecture transformation
57: 
58: ### Current
59: ```
60: [Browser] ─► Next.js App Router (35 pages)
61:               └► SQLite (single file, single user)
62: ```
63: 
64: ### Savage mode target
65: ```
66: [Browser] ◄──► Next.js (UI + SSR + REST + Server Actions)
67:    │             │
68:    │             ├─► Postgres (multi-tenant, row-level security)
69:    │             ├─► Redis (pub/sub, queue, cache, rate limits)
70:    │             ├─► BullMQ workers (cron, scheduled posts, email send)
71:    │             ├─► S3-compatible blob store (assets, exports)
72:    │             ├─► Socket.IO server (real-time)
73:    │             ├─► Provider adapters:
74:    │             │     • Resend / SMTP (email)
75:    │             │     • Twitter/X, LinkedIn, Meta, TikTok APIs
76:    │             │     • OpenAI / Anthropic / local Ollama
77:    │             │     • Stripe (billing)
78:    │             │     • Plausible / PostHog (analytics ingest)
79:    │             │     • Slack, Discord (notifications)
80:    │             ├─► Embeddable form runtime (form.js served from /embed/[id].js)
81:    │             ├─► Public REST API + webhook dispatcher
82:    │             └─► Audit log + feature flags
83:    │
84:    └─► [Embed forms running on your real website]
85: ```
86: 
87: But because we honor the **sovereign operator** principle: **all of this must run on a single machine**, with **one `docker compose up`**, with **no required external services** for the local-first experience. External providers are *opt-in* power-ups.
88: 
89: ---
90: 
91: ## 6 Phases
92: 
93: ### Phase 0 — Foundation (week 1)
94: **"Make it real, not a demo"**
95: 
96: Must do before anything else.
97: 
98: - [ ] **Auth**: magic-link + password, sessions, CSRF, httpOnly cookies
99: - [ ] **Workspaces**: multi-tenant, every table gains `workspace_id`, RLS in Postgres or app-level enforcement in SQLite
100: - [ ] **Roles**: owner / admin / editor / analyst / viewer (5 roles)

(Showing lines 1-100 of 392. Use offset=101 to continue.)
</content>

1: # BAZ Favicon — the prompt (recipe)
2: 
3: The favicon is generated from a single SVG template (the "prompt") baked into
4: `generate.py` (`SVG_MARK_TILE`), rendered via Playwright at 3× DPI, then sized
5: by Pillow into the full favicon set. Single source of truth — edit the template,
6: re-run `generate.py`, the whole set regenerates.
7: 
8: ## The recipe (`SVG_MARK_TILE` in `generate.py`)
9: 
10: ```
11: Canvas:        240 × 240
12: Background:    Midnight  #020617
13: Inner border:  thin rect, rgba(255,255,255,.08)
14: Mark:          the letter "B"
15:                font-family: Inter, sans-serif
16:                font-weight: 800
17:                fill: gold gradient  #F7E3A8 → #C29B5B → #8C6A30
18: Accent:        #22D3EE 12×12 dot, top-right
19: Corners:       square
20: ```
21: 
22: ## Brand spec (Midnight Terminal + Gold logo)
23: 
24: - **Gold (logo):** `#F7E3A8 → #C29B5B → #8C6A30`
25: - **System (Midnight Terminal):** bg `#020617` · surface `#0B1120` · signal `#22D3EE` · depth `#818CF8` · text `#F8FAFC` · muted `#94A3B8` · success `#10B981`
26: - **Fonts:** Inter (ui/body) + JetBrains Mono (metrics/tags/code)
27: - **Corners:** square (rounded-full only for pills/badges)
28: - **Motion:** ≤200ms, fade-in-up, reduced-motion aware
29: 
30: ## Regenerate
31: 
32: ```bash
33: .venv/bin/python generate.py
34: ```
35: 
36: Outputs (in `icons/`): `icon.svg`, `favicon-{16,32,48,180,512,1024}.png`, `favicon.ico` (16/32/48 multi-size), `apple-touch-icon.png` (180).
37: 
38: ## favicon.io alternative (rejected)
39: 
40: A `favicon_io.zip` pack was also generated on favicon.io (text "B", gold theme),
41: but it renders **gold-on-gold** (bg `#86724B`, letter `#6F572C`) — low contrast
42: and illegible at 16×16. The `generate.py` version (gold "B" on midnight) is the
43: canonical, high-contrast favicon. Kept here for reference only.
44: 
45: ## Live-site note
46: 
47: The deployed `public/favicon.svg` on `baz-marketing-site` is currently the
48: Midnight-Terminal **cyan** "B" (an earlier iteration, PR #10). The canonical
49: favicon per this recipe is the **gold "B" on midnight** — wire `icons/` from
50: this pack into the site to align the favicon with the gold signature already
51: shipped on the header, footer, and A4 letterhead.

(End of file - total 51 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
# BAZ Favicon — the prompt (recipe)

The favicon is generated from a single SVG template (the "prompt") baked into
`generate.py` (`SVG_MARK_TILE`), rendered via Playwright at 3× DPI, then sized
by Pillow into the full favicon set. Single source of truth — edit the template,
re-run `generate.py`, the whole set regenerates.

## The recipe (`SVG_MARK_TILE` in `generate.py`)

```
Canvas:        240 × 240
Background:    Midnight  #020617
Inner border:  thin rect, rgba(255,255,255,.08)
Mark:          the letter "B"
               font-family: Inter, sans-serif
               font-weight: 800
               fill: gold gradient  #F7E3A8 → #C29B5B → #8C6A30
Accent:        #22D3EE 12×12 dot, top-right
Corners:       square
```

1: # AGENTS.md — BAZ Marketing Hub (Tauri Desktop Shell)
2: 
3: Thin **native desktop shell** over the Next.js Marketing Hub. This repo contains
4: **no frontend** — it loads the Hub (local dev server in dev, the deployed URL in
5: production builds) inside a Tauri v2 webview window.
6: 
7: The actual Hub codebase lives at `~/marketing-hub` (Next.js + Supabase). **This
8: repo is only the shell.** Edit Hub features in `~/marketing-hub`, not here.
9: 
10: ## Stack
11: - Tauri v2 (Rust shell) — `src-tauri/`
12: - No bundled frontend; remote-frontend app (`devUrl` / `frontendDist` point at the Hub)
13: - pnpm, identifier `com.baz.marketinghub`, productName "BAZ Marketing Hub"
14: 
15: ## Config (`src-tauri/tauri.conf.json`)
16: - `build.devUrl` → `http://localhost:3000` (the local Hub dev server)
17: - `build.frontendDist` → `https://marketing-hub-roan.vercel.app` (YOUR prod Hub;
18:   from repo homepageUrl + `~/marketing-hub/.vercel` linkage; `/cockpit`/`/crm`/`/login` all 200)
19: - `build.beforeDevCommand` → starts the Hub at `:3000` if not already running
20: - Window: 1440×900, min 1024×700, centered, resizable
21: 
22: ## Prerequisites
23: - **Rust** — installed at `~/.cargo` (user-local, no sudo). Source: `. "$HOME/.cargo/env"`.
24: - **System libs (Debian 13, one-time, sudo)** — required before `tauri dev`/`build`:
25:   ```bash
26:   sudo apt install -y libwebkit2gtk-4.1-dev librsvg2-dev libgtk-3-dev \
27:     libayatana-appindicator3-dev libssl-dev build-essential pkg-config libxdo-dev
28:   ```
29: - **pnpm** — the shell project. Note: a stray `~/pnpm-workspace.yaml` hijacks pnpm
30:   workspace-root detection, so this project ships its own `pnpm-workspace.yaml`
31:   (`packages: []`) to be a self-contained root. Do not remove it.
32: 
33: ## Commands
34: - `pnpm tauri dev` — launches the Hub (if down) and opens the native window over `localhost:3000`.
35: - `pnpm tauri build` — builds the desktop binary; loads the prod Hub URL at runtime.
36: - `pnpm tauri info` — print config/diagnostics (no webview/Rust build needed).
37: 
38: ## "Do it properly" — next phase (unified cross-platform update pipeline)
39: Goal: one version source + signed update manifest pushing to **site/hub (Vercel)**,
40: **Tauri desktop (updater plugin)**, and **phone (Capacitor today → Tauri v2 mobile
41: candidate)**. Roadmap:
42: 1. **Prod URL confirmed** — `https://marketing-hub-roan.vercel.app` is the real
43:    Hub (repo homepageUrl; Vercel project `marketing-hub` prj_8LMclxJVzf3IlQvU7ew3fPanjJev;
44:    `/cockpit`/`/crm`/`/login` all 200). The bare `marketing-hub.vercel.app` belongs
45:    to someone else. Keep prod synced by pushing `main` (Vercel auto-deploy).
46:    - **Update economics:** the shell loads the live URL, so Hub/site updates need
47:      **zero shell-side release** — only native-shell changes (window, plugins,
48:      Rust) need a Tauri updater push. Phone (Capacitor) needs store/OTA. One
49:      semver source; three delivery rails.
50: 2. **Webview auth** — cookie/session scope for the prod origin inside Tauri's
51:    WebKit; the Hub's stateless HMAC sessions help. Likely need token-in-header
52:    auth for the desktop client (3rd-party-cookie limits). Scope a capability for
53:    the remote origin if we inject Tauri APIs into the Hub.
54: 3. **Native affordances** (Tauri plugins) — app menu, system tray, global hotkey,
55:    notifications, **biometric unlock** (mirror the Capacitor cockpit), deep links,
56:    FS import/export of assets/reports.
57: 4. **Unified updater** — Tauri `updater` plugin (signed keypair, JSON manifest on
58:    a static host) for desktop; wire the Hub's deploy to bump the manifest version.
59:    Phone: decide Capacitor-vs-Tauri-mobile live-update (Capacitor via store;
60:    Tauri mobile via same updater pipeline). One semver source for all three.
61: 5. **Brand** — Midnight Terminal window chrome + icon set + splash; CSP hardening
62:    (currently `null`).
63: 6. **CSP / security** — set a real CSP allowing the Hub origin; lock capabilities
64:    to the remote origin; drop `csp: null`.
65: 
66: ## Scaffold status
67: - **Tier 1 — DONE (code, unverified-compile):** stripped the `tauri init` template
68:   (`greet` boilerplate removed); native **app menu** (BAZ app menu + Window menu,
69:   Reload/Fullscreen/Quit/Minimize/Close) + **system tray** (icon, Reload/Quit,
70:   left-click shows & focuses the window) wired in `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` using only
71:   tauri-core APIs (no plugins) verified against tauri **2.11.5**. **CSP hardened**
72:   in `tauri.conf.json` (was `null`): scoped to the prod Hub origin + Supabase +
73:   Vercel, `frame-ancestors 'none'`, `object-src 'none'`. Capabilities left as-is
74:   (no `remote` block → the remote Hub has NO access to `window.__TAURI__` IPC by
75:   default = secure). `Cargo.toml` unchanged (menu/tray are core, no new deps).
76: - **BUILD BLOCKER (env, not code):** all Tauri Linux `-dev` prereqs are missing
77:   (`libwebkit2gtk-4.1-dev libgtk-3-dev librsvg2-dev libglib2.0-dev libssl-dev
78:   libxdo-dev build-essential pkg-config`). Build dies at `glib-sys`/`gio-sys`/
79:   `gobject-sys` (pkg-config can't find `glib-2.0`). Fix = the apt one-liner in
80:   Prerequisites. `lib.rs` compile-validation is pending this install.
81: - **CSP caveat:** for a remote `frontendDist` the webview loads the Hub over https
82:   directly, so `app.security.csp` is **inert** for the live Hub (the Hub's own
83:   Vercel CSP governs); it becomes binding only if we ever bundle the frontend.
84:   `connect-src` `https://*`/`wss://*` wildcards must be tightened to the Hub's
85:   real origins after a network audit pre-prod.
86: - **Tier 2 — NEXT (needs compile-check):** `tauri-plugin-global-shortcut`,
87:   `tauri-plugin-notification`, `tauri-plugin-updater` (signed keypair + manifest
88:   host). Wire after the apt install so plugin APIs can be `cargo check`-ed against
89:   downloaded sources. DevTools menu item gated behind the `devtools` cargo feature.
90: 
91: ## Risks / assumptions
92: - [fact] Prod Hub URL is `https://marketing-hub-roan.vercel.app` (yours; confirmed
93:   via repo homepageUrl + `.vercel` linkage + route probes). The bare
94:   `marketing-hub.vercel.app` is someone else's project. DEPLOY.md's
95:   `roi-marketing.vercel.app` serves "Pivot Studio" — stale/wrong, ignore it.
96: - [assumption] `marketing-hub.vercel.app` auto-deploys from `main`; verify before
97:   relying on it for production desktop builds.
98: - [risk] Remote-frontend shell depends on the Hub being reachable at runtime;
99:   no offline bundle today (service worker caches visited routes only).
100: - This shell does **not** duplicate the Hub — it is a viewport. Do not rebuild
101:   Hub features here.

(End of file - total 101 lines)
</content>

1: /**
2:  * The Content Machine — Gary Vaynerchuk's framework codified.
3:  *
4:  * Core idea: "Document, don't create." One big moment → 30+ platform-native
5:  * derivative assets. The marketer's job shifts from making content to
6:  * CAPTURING content, then the machine multiplies it.
7:  *
8:  * Philosophy (Gary Vee):
9:  *   - Attention is the only稀缺 resource. Buy it with volume, not polish.
10:  *   - Jab, jab, jab, right hook. Give value at scale. Then ask once.
11:  *   - One piece of pillar content per week is enough. The work is the
12:  *     repurpose, not the original recording.
13:  *   - Platform-native ≠ cross-post. LinkedIn ≠ Twitter ≠ Instagram. Each
14:  *     format rewards different shapes. The machine respects the difference.
15:  *   - Document the work you're already doing. The market is starving for
16:  *     authenticity, not produced content.
17:  */
18: 
19: export type Platform =
20:   | "linkedin_post"
21:   | "linkedin_carousel"
22:   | "twitter_thread"
23:   | "twitter_post"
24:   | "instagram_carousel"
25:   | "instagram_reel"
26:   | "youtube_short"
27:   | "tiktok"
28:   | "newsletter"
29:   | "blog_post"
30:   | "podcast_clip";

33:   | "podcast"
34:   | "keynote"
35:   | "customer_call"
36:   | "blog_post"
37:   | "video"
38:   | "note"
39:   | "meeting"
40:   | "interview"
41:   | "screen_recording";

4:    override the monochrome layer.
5:    Electric cyan accent + deep-midnight bg + ultraviolet depth. Square corners.

7: :root {
8:   /* ── Accent / brand → electric cyan ── */
9:   --accent: #22D3EE !important;

11:   --accent-glow: hsla(189, 94%, 53%, 0.40) !important;

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Three Pricing Cards
2: 
3: Create a pricing section as one vertically stacked block on a full-width background inside a max-width container. At the top, place a centered section heading on its own line; directly below the heading, a centered supporting paragraph at a comfortable reading width.
4: 
5: Below the heading block, place a horizontal row of three pricing cards of equal width with consistent gutters between them. Inside each card, stack from top to bottom: a plan name on its own line; a single-line plan description directly below the name; a large dominant price paired with a small "/month" suffix sitting on the same baseline to the right of the amount; a thin horizontal divider spanning the full card width; a vertical list of feature rows (each row composed of a small circular check icon anchored at the left and a short feature label to its right on the same baseline); and one full-width primary call-to-action button whose label text is centered within the button, placed at the bottom of the card.
6: 
7: On narrow viewports the three cards stack vertically in their original left-to-right reading order; each card stretches to the full content width with comfortable side padding.

(End of file - total 7 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
# Three Pricing Cards

Create a pricing section as one vertically stacked block on a full-width background inside a max-width container. At the top, place a centered section heading on its own line; directly below the heading, a centered supporting paragraph at a comfortable reading width.

Below the heading block, place a horizontal row of three pricing cards of equal width with consistent gutters between them. Inside each card, stack from top to bottom: a plan name on its own line; a single-line plan description directly below the name; a large dominant price paired with a small "/month" suffix sitting on the same baseline to the right of the amount; a thin horizontal divider spanning the full card width; a vertical list of feature rows (each row composed of a small circular check icon anchored at the left and a short feature label to its right on the same baseline); and one full-width primary call-to-action button whose label text is centered within the button, placed at the bottom of the card.

On narrow viewports the three cards stack vertically in their original left-to-right reading order; each card stretches to the full content width with comfortable side padding.

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Eight Feature Cards Grid
2: 
3: Create a feature section inside a centered page container with comfortable side padding. At the top, place a centered header block composed of a large heading centered on its own line followed by a centered supporting paragraph directly beneath it (constrain the paragraph to a moderate reading width).
4: 
5: Below the header, place a responsive grid of eight feature cards arranged as four columns and two rows on the widest breakpoint, with consistent gutters between all cards. Each card is its own rounded rectangular panel with a thin outer border. Inside each card, stack from top to bottom: a small rounded-square icon container at the top (holding a single centered glyph); a bold short title on its own line directly below the icon; a left-aligned supporting paragraph directly below the title that fills the remaining card width with comfortable internal padding on all sides.
6: 
7: On smaller breakpoints, reduce the column count stepwise (four columns, then two, then one) until the cards form a single column on the narrowest viewports; preserve left-to-right top-to-bottom reading order throughout.

(End of file - total 7 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
# Eight Feature Cards Grid

Create a feature section inside a centered page container with comfortable side padding. At the top, place a centered header block composed of a large heading centered on its own line followed by a centered supporting paragraph directly beneath it (constrain the paragraph to a moderate reading width).

Below the header, place a responsive grid of eight feature cards arranged as four columns and two rows on the widest breakpoint, with consistent gutters between all cards. Each card is its own rounded rectangular panel with a thin outer border. Inside each card, stack from top to bottom: a small rounded-square icon container at the top (holding a single centered glyph); a bold short title on its own line directly below the icon; a left-aligned supporting paragraph directly below the title that fills the remaining card width with comfortable internal padding on all sides.

On smaller breakpoints, reduce the column count stepwise (four columns, then two, then one) until the cards form a single column on the narrowest viewports; preserve left-to-right top-to-bottom reading order throughout.

1: /**
2:  * Blog seed posts — derived from the Lexicon.
3:  * Each post is a long-form essay (markdown-ish) that introduces a concept,
4:  * explains who coined it, gives a quick playbook, and CTAs to /leads.
5:  *
6:  * Hand-authored so they read like a real founder blog, not generated fluff.
7:  */
8: 
9: export interface BlogPost {
10:   slug: string;
11:   title: string;
12:   excerpt: string; // 1-2 sentence teaser
13:   category:
14:     "strategy" | "copy" | "tribe" | "growth" | "brand" | "pricing" | "analytics" | "automation";
15:   author: string; // Display name
16:   publishedAt: number; // ms epoch
17:   readMinutes: number;
18:   tags: string[];
19:   body: string; // markdown-lite (paragraphs + headings + lists)
20:   relatedLexicon?: string[]; // slugs
21: }
22: 
23: export const BLOG_POSTS: BlogPost[] = [
24:   {
25:     slug: "stp-the-spine-of-every-winning-campaign",
26:     title: "STP: the spine of every campaign that ever won",
27:     excerpt:
28:       "Most marketing fails at the targeting step. Trying to be for everyone means being for no one. Here's the 60-year-old framework that still separates winners from noise.",
29:     category: "strategy",
30:     author: "Brahim · BAZ Empire",
31:     publishedAt: Date.now() - 1000 * 60 * 60 * 24 * 7, // 7 days ago
32:     readMinutes: 5,
33:     tags: ["strategy", "STP", "Kotler", "positioning"],
34:     relatedLexicon: ["stp", "positioning"],
35:     body: `Most marketing fails at the targeting step. Trying to be for everyone means being for no one.
36: 
37: ## What STP actually is
38: 
39: Philip Kotler gave us three letters in the 1960s and they remain the spine of every campaign that ever won:
40: 
41: - **Segmentation.** Divide the market into groups of people with similar needs. Not by demographics first — by what they're trying to do, what they're afraid of, what would make them cry with relief.
42: - **Targeting.** Pick the one group you can win. The one you can be the obvious choice for, with the resources you have, in the time you have.
43: - **Positioning.** Write the one sentence that makes them choose you. The promise in their language, not yours.
44: 
45: ## Why most teams skip it
46: 
47: Because it's hard. Segmentation requires you to say "no" to people you could serve. Targeting means watching competitors take the rest. Positioning means condensing your entire worldview into a sentence your customer says back to you at dinner.
48: 
49: So most teams skip straight to tactics. They run ads, write copy, build landing pages. They confuse motion for progress. Three months in, the metrics are flat and they hire another agency.
50: 
51: ## The 90-minute STP workshop
52: 
53: Pick the next campaign. Spend 90 minutes on STP before any tactics. Use this template:
54: 
55: 1. List 10 customers who love you. Why? What did they look like before they found you?
56: 2. Cluster them. You'll find 2-3 segments, not 10.
57: 3. Pick one. The one where you have the most unfair advantage.
58: 4. Write the positioning in their words. "We help [segment] do [dream outcome] without [sacrifice]."
59: 
60: That's it. Tactics will get 10x easier. Your cost of acquisition will halve. You'll know which channels to ignore.
61: 
62: ## The BAZ Empire take
63: 
64: The Marketing Hub's Cockpit scores your STP fit on the Patrick Number. Run a free audit at [/leads](/leads) — we'll show you which segment your current copy is actually written for, and which one it should be.`,
65:   },
66:   {
67:     slug: "permission-marketing-still-wins",
68:     title: "Permission marketing still wins (and interruption is dying faster than you think)",
69:     excerpt:
70:       "Seth Godin wrote the book on permission in 1999. Twenty-six years later, every platform is forcing it back into your funnel. Here's the playbook.",
71:     category: "growth",
72:     author: "Brahim · BAZ Empire",
73:     publishedAt: Date.now() - 1000 * 60 * 60 * 24 * 5,
74:     readMinutes: 4,
75:     tags: ["permission", "Seth Godin", "email", "list-building"],
76:     relatedLexicon: ["permission-marketing", "smallest-viable-audience"],
77:     body: `Seth Godin wrote *Permission Marketing* in 1999. The thesis was simple: interruption is a loan, not income. Eventually the lender calls it.
78: 
79: Twenty-six years later, the loan has been called. Safari kills third-party cookies. iOS kills email pixels. YouTube kills pre-roll. TikTok kills anything that doesn't grab in 1.2 seconds. RSS, the original permission channel, has quietly outlived three of its killers.
80: 
81: ## The math of interruption vs permission
82: 
83: - **Interruption.** Reach 100,000. Convert 0.5%. Pay $20 CAC. Earn $50 LTV. Margin: razor-thin. One platform change and you're negative.
84: - **Permission.** Reach 5,000 (your list). Convert 8%. Pay $1.50 CAC (just the email tool). Earn $120 LTV (because you earned the right to upsell). Margin: fat. The platform can't take it away.
85: 
86: ## The 4-step permission playbook
87: 
88: 1. **Earn the first attention.** A free thing of real value — not a PDF of "5 tips," but something they'd actually pay for.
89: 2. **Deliver more than promised.** Over-deliver on day one so day two feels safe.
90: 3. **Ask for the next step.** A second free thing. A category. A small commitment. Don't jump to the sale.
91: 4. **Treat the list like a garden.** Segment by what they read. Remove people who never open. Water the ones who do.
92: 
93: ## What we built
94:

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Centered Login Card Page
2: 
3: Create a login page as one centered vertical stack on a full-page background. At the very top, place a small brand mark composed of a circular logo glyph followed by a short brand wordmark, both centered horizontally on the page. Below the brand mark, place one centered form card with rounded corners and a moderate max width. Inside the card, stack from top to bottom: a card heading on its own line; a labeled email input row (the label sits above the input on its own line and pairs a short label text with a tiny required-field marker to the right of the label; the input includes a small leading icon glyph inside the field); a labeled password input row in the same shape directly below (also carrying a small leading icon glyph inside the field); a single horizontal row composed of a small square "remember me" checkbox paired with an inline label on the left and a "forgot password" text link on the right; one full-width primary submit button beneath the checkbox row; finally, a small centered helper line containing a short prompt sentence followed by an inline text link to the sign-up flow. Treat the area outside the card as a subtle decorative field (for example a sparse dot or grid pattern). On narrow viewports keep the same single-column stack inside the card and let the card occupy the full content width with comfortable side padding.

(End of file - total 3 lines)
</content>

<content>
1: # Split Marketing Login Page
2: 
3: Create a login page split into two columns on large 1280px width breakpoints inside a centered page container with comfortable side padding. The left column is a marketing column carrying copy and a short feature list; the right column is the form column. The two columns share the same vertical center line.
4: 
5: The **left column** is a left-aligned vertical stack containing, in order: a large multi-line heading on its own line; a supporting paragraph below the heading at a comfortable reading width; a vertical list of about three feature rows each composed of a small leading icon at the left, a bold one-line title to the right of the icon on the same baseline, and a short supporting line directly beneath the title that sits flush-left under the icon-and-title row.
6: 
7: The **right column** is a left-aligned vertical stack containing, in order: a heading on its own line; a horizontal row of two equal third-party provider buttons sitting side by side (each button carries a small leading provider glyph and a short provider label); a horizontal "or" separator made of a thin rule, a centered short word, and another thin rule; a two-column field grid containing two labeled inputs on one row (email input on the left and password input on the right) — every label sits above its input with a tiny required-field marker to the right of the label text; a single horizontal row composed of a small square "remember me" checkbox paired with an inline label on the left and a "forgot password" text link on the right; one full-width primary submit button beneath the checkbox row; finally a small helper line containing a short prompt sentence followed by an inline text link to the sign-up flow.
8: 
9: On narrow viewports, stack with the form column first and the marketing column second so the user reaches the form quickly; keep all internal stacks in their original reading order.

(End of file - total 9 lines)
</content>

1: # Centered Forgot Password Card
2: 
3: Create a forgot-password page as one centered vertical stack on a full-width section background that may include a very subtle repeating dot or grid texture behind the content, vertically centered within the viewport height. Stack from top to bottom: a small brand icon or logo mark centered horizontally on its own line paired with a brand name label immediately to the right of the icon on the same baseline; a single elevated card with rounded corners placed directly below the brand row, centered horizontally and constrained to a comfortable form width. Inside the card, stack from top to bottom: a bold heading on its own line (e.g. "Forgot your password?"); a supporting paragraph of one or two sentences directly below the heading at a comfortable reading width, reassuring the user and explaining the reset flow; a labeled email input row placed below the paragraph (the label sits above the input on its own line with a tiny required-field marker to the right of the label text; the input carries a leading envelope icon affordance flush-left inside the field and a placeholder suggesting an email address); a single checkbox row containing a small square checkbox at the left and an inline label whose text confirms acceptance of linked terms and conditions (with the terms phrase rendered as an inline text link); one full-width primary action button at the bottom of the card with a short label (e.g. "Reset password").
4: 
5: On narrow viewports keep the same single-column centered stack with comfortable side padding; allow the card to span the full content width while preserving center alignment throughout.

(End of file - total 5 lines)
</content>

1: # Featured Middle Pricing Card
2: 
3: Create a pricing section as one vertically stacked block inside a max-width container. At the top, place a centered heading on its own line; directly below the heading, a centered supporting paragraph at a comfortable reading width.
4: 
5: Below the heading block, place a horizontal row of three pricing cards of equal width. The middle card is visually elevated (taller than the two side cards, sitting on a distinct filled background) and carries a small "Most popular" pill badge anchored to the center of its top edge. Inside each card, stack from top to bottom: a plan name on its own line; a short single-line description directly below the name; a large dominant price with a small "/month" suffix on the same baseline to the right of the amount; one full-width primary call-to-action button (the middle card's button may use a contrasting treatment to reinforce its featured status); and a vertical list of feature rows (each row composed of a small circular check icon at the left and a short feature label to its right on the same baseline).
6: 
7: On narrow viewports the three cards stack vertically with the highlighted middle card preserving its position in the sequence; each card stretches to the full content width with comfortable side padding.

(End of file - total 7 lines)
</content>
